items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.

items.correct: Water vapour released from plant leaves to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: Water flowing through soil into rivers.
items.why: Students confuse transpiration with other water movements. It's specifically water LOSS THROUGH PLANTS - significant in forested areas, contributing substantially to atmospheric water vapour over land.

items.q: Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1968. “1962” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Brundtland definition of sustainable development appeared in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1987. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year of the Rio Earth Summit?
items.why: The real figure is 1992 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
